project.name: Ad Data Ingestion Pipeline
project.scenario: The marketing team runs ads on 10+ media platforms and tunes budgets daily on spend, CTR, and CPA — the fresher the numbers, the better the calls.
project.pain: Data was pulled by hand from each platform's console and stitched into spreadsheets: 10+ hours a week, and at best day-old numbers — always making today's decisions on yesterday's data.
project.now: Every hour the pipeline pulls from all platforms, normalizes and dedupes the data, and stores it in a database. The team self-serves on a BI dashboard — manual exports no longer exist.

scheduler.name: Scheduler
scheduler.role: Kicks off an ad-data pull every hour, on the hour — no human needed.
impact: Data freshness went from daily to hourly; the numbers the team sees are at most an hour old.
No one runs scripts by hand anymore — that is where the 10+ saved hours a week come from.
scheduler.how: On the hour, AWS wakes a small piece of code that does exactly one thing: tell the Fetch Layer to start pulling. Then it sleeps until the next hour.
scheduler.fail: If one run fails, that hour's data has a gap; the next run fires as usual — nothing cascades.

code.n: Tells AWS: when there is work, run the function src/ingest.run.
This line is the timer itself. cron(0 * * * ? *) means: fire at minute 0 of every hour.

code.c:
// src/ingest.ts
export const run = async () => pipeline.start();
code.n: The only thing it does when woken: start the pipeline.

scheduler.tourHint: Stop 1 · Starts the whole pipeline every hour — hourly freshness is decided right here.

fetch.name: Fetch Layer
fetch.role: Pulls raw ad data from 10+ media platforms.
impact: 10+ platforms, each with its own way of connecting — the most maintenance-heavy part of the project.
If one platform changes its API, only that plugin breaks; the rest keep running.
Adding a new platform means writing one new plugin from the template — nothing else changes.
fetch.how: One small plugin per platform, all the same shape: a single getData(params) interface (an API is simply an agreed way for two programs to talk). When the Scheduler fires, every plugin pulls from its own platform.
fetch.fail: If a platform's API is down or changed, only that platform's data is missing; the rest keep running, and the gap is backfilled next round.

code.n: One file like this per platform — it registers who it is.
Every plugin implements the same getData — the rest of the code never cares how differently TikTok and Google behave.
code.risk: ⚠ This line makes a real API call and spends your quota on that platform. The rate limiter at the Redis stop exists for this line.

tradeoff.cost: Every platform's integration details are yours to maintain; when a platform changes, you patch.
tradeoff.when: With only 2-3 major platforms and stable SDKs, SDKs are less work. With 10+ platforms and one unified interface, adaptors win — the case here.

fetch.tourHint: Stop 2 · Pulls raw data from 10+ platforms — the most maintenance-heavy part.

normalize.name: Normalize Layer
normalize.role: Unifies each platform's differently-named fields into one standard JSON format.
impact: "Impressions" on the dashboard always means one thing, whichever platform the data came from.
However odd a new platform's fields are, changes stay inside this layer — nothing downstream moves.
normalize.how: Platforms name the same metric differently: some say views, some say impressions. This layer renames fields against one mapping table, then passes the data on.
normalize.fail: A field missing from the mapping table is skipped and logged — unrecognized data is never passed downstream.

code.n: The mapping table itself: platform names on the left, unified field names on the right.

code.c:
export const normalize = (src, row) =>
  mapKeys(row, FIELD_MAP[src]);
code.n: The whole operation is one line — swap field names per the table.

normalize.tourHint: Stop 3 · Field names get unified here; downstream never cares where data came from.

redis.name: Redis cache & rate limit
redis.role: Drops duplicate data and throttles calls to each platform's API so you don't get banned.
impact: The same ad record never enters the database twice, so report numbers don't inflate.
No API key gets banned for calling too hard — one ban can mean days of missing data.
redis.how: Each record gets a fingerprint; Redis checks whether that fingerprint was seen before and drops it if so. Before every API call, the hour's call count is checked — over quota means waiting for the next hour.
redis.fail: If Redis goes down, the pipeline pauses rather than running unprotected — hammering APIs with no rate limit is worse than losing an hour.

code.n: Dedup in three lines. NX means "write only if never seen" — a failed write means a duplicate.

code.c:
const used = await redis.incr('quota:' + platform + ':' + hour);
if (used > LIMIT) await sleepUntilNextHour();
code.n: Rate limiting is just counting: which call of the hour is this; over the line, wait.

tradeoff.a: Redis for dedup and rate limiting
tradeoff.cost: One more component to run — extra memory and operational overhead.
tradeoff.when: At small volume (a few thousand rows a day) a unique index is enough and Redis can be cut. At 10+ platforms pulled hourly, the volume justifies it.

redis.tourHint: Stop 4 · Dedup + rate limiting — this keeps your API keys alive.

db.name: Database
db.role: Stores the cleaned ad data and serves the Dashboard's queries.
impact: Every number on the Dashboard is queried from here — if it's down, reports go blank.
Data is stored by day: "last 7 days" is fast, "all of last year" is slow. That's the current design's boundary.
db.how: Data cleared by Redis is written row by row into the ads_daily table; Looker Studio connects directly and runs aggregate queries (the kind that sums many rows into one number on a chart).
db.fail: If the database is unreachable, the batch waits in a queue for retry — nothing is lost; the Dashboard shows stale data meanwhile.

code.c:
CREATE TABLE ads_daily (
  platform    text,
  ad_id       text,
  date        date,
  impressions bigint,
  spend       numeric,
  PRIMARY KEY (platform, ad_id, date)
);
code.n: The entire store is one table. The three-part primary key guarantees one row per ad per day — the last line of defense for dedup.
code.risk: ⚠ Changing this table's structure (columns, primary key) is the hardest thing in the project to undo — everything is built on it. Back up first.

tradeoff.cost: Past hundreds of millions of rows, large aggregate queries struggle.
tradeoff.when: When the team runs "two years, all platforms" analyses or rows pass 100M, move to BigQuery. Until then PostgreSQL is cheaper and simpler.

db.tourHint: Stop 5 · Where the data finally lives — every Dashboard number is queried from here.

dash.name: BI Dashboard
dash.role: Lets the marketing team see spend, CTR, and CPA directly — no SQL, no waiting for exports.
impact: The team self-serves; you never manually export a weekly report again.
This part is bought, not built — zero lines of frontend code.
dash.how: Looker Studio connects straight to PostgreSQL. You configure charts by drag-and-drop; it generates the queries.
dash.fail: When a chart errors, the problem is almost always upstream — check whether today's data is in the Database before suspecting the chart config.
dash.tourHint: Stop 6 · The team self-serves here — the pipeline closes its loop.
